title: Yosemite National Park Equal Access and Fairness Act
short_title: Yosemite National Park Equal Access and Fairness Act
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
plain_summary: This bill changes a 1913 law that let San Francisco build a dam in Yosemite National Park. The bill makes San Francisco pay much more rent each year, going from $30,000 to $2 million, for using the park's water areas. The money will help pay for preventing wildfires in California's national parks. The bill also requires the National Park Service to let more people use these water areas for fun activities like swimming and boating while still protecting the natural beauty of the park.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Yosemite National Park Equal Access and Fairness Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Expansion of Access to Hetch Hetchy Reservoir and Lake Eleanor Basin Areas for Recreational Purposes
sections.text:
This section changes an old law from nineteen thirteen. The changes increase the yearly payment that San Francisco must make to use these areas from thirty thousand dollars to two million dollars. This payment must go up each year based on how much prices rise for everyday items that city people buy. San Francisco cannot make their water or power customers pay for this fee. The city can also use this money for preventing and fighting wildfires.

The law also adds new rules for fun activities at Hetch Hetchy Reservoir and Lake Eleanor Basin. The Secretary of the Interior, who runs the National Park Service, must manage these areas so people can enjoy them for recreation. The Secretary must try to keep and improve these areas for public fun while protecting their natural beauty, history, and science value.

People can do these activities in these areas if the Secretary says it is okay and if the activities do not hurt the main uses of the areas. People can swim and use boats without motors. They can camp around the areas but not below the normal high water line. They can have picnics. The Secretary can allow other fun activities too. People can drive cars and motorcycles on roads to get to these areas if their driving does not hurt the dams or related buildings.

Within one year of this law passing, the Secretary must write a report for Congress. The report must study whether the original nineteen thirteen law about public access to these areas has been followed since they were created. The report must also study how to collect money and spend funds to give fair access to these areas for everyone. This includes looking at costs for trail care, road fixes, and wildfire prevention and fighting. The report must consider ways to pay for these costs, such as charging more for water and power from the Hetch Hetchy system, treating San Francisco like any other business that operates in national parks, and adding to San Francisco's yearly payment the value of benefits lost when these areas were flooded, including recreation and flood control.
